title: Art and Commerce
summary: An artist and a gallery owner negotiate over pricing, commission rates, and creative control as they consider a long-term partnership.

body.speaker: Artist
body.text: I appreciate you reaching out, Oliver. Your gallery has a reputation for placing work with serious collectors.

body.speaker: Gallery Owner
body.text: And your last series attracted considerable critical attention, which is exactly the kind of momentum we like to build on.

body.speaker: Artist
body.text: I'd love to show here, but I want to be upfront about a few things before we go any further.

body.speaker: Gallery Owner
body.text: Of course. This kind of conversation is far better to have now than after a contract is signed.

body.speaker: Artist
body.text: My concern is commission. Forty-five percent feels steep when I'm covering my own materials, studio rental, and framing.

body.speaker: Gallery Owner
body.text: Our standard rate reflects significant overheads — insurance, staffing, private view events, and an extensive collector network that most emerging artists simply don't have access to.

body.speaker: Artist
body.text: I understand the value of the network. What I'm proposing is a sliding scale: thirty-five percent on works priced under five thousand, forty on anything above.

body.speaker: Gallery Owner
body.text: A sliding scale is unusual for us, but the logic isn't unreasonable. Could you meet us at thirty-eight flat? That's a meaningful reduction from forty-five.

body.speaker: Artist
body.text: I could accept thirty-eight if you commit to a minimum of two group shows and one solo exhibition within the first eighteen months.

body.speaker: Gallery Owner
body.text: The solo show is ambitious for eighteen months. We typically plan exhibitions twelve to fourteen months in advance.

body.speaker: Artist
body.text: Then let's say twenty-four months for the solo, with a group show confirmed within six. That gives both of us time to gauge audience response.

body.speaker: Gallery Owner
body.text: That's workable. I'd also want a right of first refusal on your next two bodies of work after this agreement begins.

body.speaker: Artist
body.text: Right of first refusal is fine, but I need to retain creative control. I've had experiences where commercial pressure shifted my practice in directions I regretted.

body.speaker: Gallery Owner
body.text: We never dictate subject matter. What we do offer is curatorial guidance — suggestions about scale, sequencing, how a body of work reads in a white-cube context.

body.speaker: Artist
body.text: Curatorial input I welcome. What I need written into the contract is that final decisions on titles, pricing, and display remain mine.

body.speaker: Gallery Owner
body.text: Pricing I'd want to be a joint decision, given that it affects the gallery's positioning in the market.

body.speaker: Artist
body.text: I can agree to joint pricing decisions, provided there's a written floor below which we won't sell regardless of market conditions. I don't want my work underpriced to move inventory quickly.

body.speaker: Gallery Owner
body.text: A price floor is actually in our interest too — it protects the resale value for existing collectors. I think we're very close to an agreement here.

body.speaker: Artist
body.text: I'm glad. There's one last thing: I'd like the right to display up to three works per year outside the gallery, at art fairs or institutional shows, without commission.

body.speaker: Gallery Owner
body.text: Three non-commission works annually is acceptable, provided they carry gallery attribution. Visibility benefits us both.

annotations.meaningVi: Making contact with someone for a professional or collaborative purpose.
annotations.exampleEn: Thank you for reaching out — I've admired your work for years.

annotations.meaningVi: Serious notice and commentary from art critics or media reviewers.
annotations.exampleEn: Her debut show received unexpected critical attention.

annotations.meaningVi: The force or energy gained from a series of successes.
annotations.exampleEn: Winning the award gave her career significant momentum.

annotations.meaningVi: Honest and direct about something from the start.
annotations.exampleEn: I'd rather be upfront about my concerns than surprise you later.

annotations.meaningVi: The percentage of a sale price that a gallery keeps as payment for selling artwork.
annotations.exampleEn: A forty-percent commission is common in commercial galleries.

annotations.meaningVi: Surprisingly high, especially in price.
annotations.exampleEn: Forty-five percent feels steep given my production costs.

annotations.meaningVi: The regular, unavoidable costs of running a business (rent, insurance, salaries).
annotations.exampleEn: High overheads mean the gallery needs a significant commission to survive.

annotations.meaningVi: An exclusive opening event for an exhibition, held before it opens to the general public.
annotations.exampleEn: Collectors received invitations to the private view.

annotations.meaningVi: A system where rates change based on a variable such as price or income.
annotations.exampleEn: A sliding scale commission rewards higher-priced sales.

annotations.meaningVi: A single fixed rate that does not vary.
annotations.exampleEn: We charge a flat fee of thirty-eight percent on all works.

annotations.meaningVi: An exhibition featuring the work of more than one artist.
annotations.exampleEn: Appearing in a group show helped her gain visibility.

annotations.meaningVi: An exhibition featuring work by a single artist.
annotations.exampleEn: A solo exhibition is a major milestone for any artist's career.

annotations.meaningVi: To assess or measure something, often a reaction or level.
annotations.exampleEn: We need time to gauge the collector's interest.

annotations.meaningVi: The contractual right to be offered something before it is offered to others.
annotations.exampleEn: The gallery has a right of first refusal on her next series.

annotations.meaningVi: The authority to make final decisions about the artistic direction of one's work.
annotations.exampleEn: She signed the deal only after securing full creative control.

annotations.meaningVi: Professional advice about how to select, arrange, and present artwork for an exhibition.
annotations.exampleEn: Curatorial guidance helped the show feel coherent rather than chaotic.

annotations.meaningVi: The aesthetic of a typical modern gallery — white walls, neutral space — and how art reads within it.
annotations.exampleEn: Large paintings need space to breathe in a white-cube context.

annotations.meaningVi: How a brand or product is perceived relative to competitors in a market.
annotations.exampleEn: Low prices could damage the gallery's positioning among top collectors.

annotations.meaningVi: The minimum price below which a work will not be sold.
annotations.exampleEn: A price floor prevents panic selling during a market downturn.

annotations.meaningVi: The price a work of art can achieve when it is sold on by its first buyer.
annotations.exampleEn: Selling too cheaply now could harm the resale value of her early pieces.

annotations.meaningVi: The credit given to identify who created or represents a work.
annotations.exampleEn: All works displayed outside the gallery must carry gallery attribution.

annotations.meaningVi: A collection of artworks created as part of a single creative project or period.
annotations.exampleEn: Her new body of work explores themes of memory and loss.

grammarNotes.title: Fronted objects for emphasis (topicalisation)
grammarNotes.bodyVi: In natural spoken English, speakers sometimes move the object to the front of the sentence to signal what they are responding to or what matters most to them. 'Curatorial input I welcome' and 'Pricing I'd want to be a joint decision' both front the object. This is called topicalisation. It is more common in spoken and literary registers than in formal writing. The inversion signals: 'here is the specific thing I am addressing; let me state my position on it'. Examples: Curatorial input I welcome. / Pricing I'd want to be a joint decision, given that it affects the gallery's positioning in the market.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: In natural spoken English, speakers sometimes move the object to the front of the sentence to signal what they are responding to or what matters most to them. 'Curatorial input I welcome' and 'Pricing I'd want to be a joint decision' both front the object. This is called topicalisation. It is more common in spoken and literary registers than in formal writing. The inversion signals: 'here is the specific thing I am addressing; let me state my position on it'. Examples: Curatorial input I welcome. / Pricing I'd want to be a joint decision, given that it affects the gallery's positioning in the market.

grammarNotes.title: Conditional clauses with 'provided' and 'provided that'
grammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Provided' and 'provided that' function as more formal, emphatic alternatives to 'if'. They introduce a condition that must be met for the main clause to apply. 'I could accept thirty-eight if you commit…' and 'I could accept thirty-eight provided you commit…' carry the same logical meaning, but 'provided' emphasises that the condition is non-negotiable — it must be satisfied for the offer to stand. This makes it common in contracts and high-stakes negotiations. Examples: I can agree to joint pricing decisions, provided there's a written floor below which we won't sell. / Three non-commission works annually is acceptable, provided they carry gallery attribution.
grammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Provided' and 'provided that' function as more formal, emphatic alternatives to 'if'. They introduce a condition that must be met for the main clause to apply. 'I could accept thirty-eight if you commit…' and 'I could accept thirty-eight provided you commit…' carry the same logical meaning, but 'provided' emphasises that the condition is non-negotiable — it must be satisfied for the offer to stand. This makes it common in contracts and high-stakes negotiations. Examples: I can agree to joint pricing decisions, provided there's a written floor below which we won't sell. / Three non-commission works annually is acceptable, provided they carry gallery attribution.

questions.prompt: Why does the Artist want to discuss terms before signing anything?
options: She has already signed with another gallery
She wants to be upfront about concerns from the start
She is testing the gallery's patience
She does not trust the Gallery Owner
questions.explanation: She says 'I want to be upfront about a few things before we go any further'.
questions.hint: Listen for what the Artist says about wanting to be transparent.

questions.prompt: What is the Gallery Owner's standard commission rate?
questions.explanation: The Artist describes 'forty-five percent' as the gallery's current rate and calls it steep.
questions.hint: Listen for the first percentage figure the Artist mentions.

questions.prompt: What sliding scale does the Artist propose?
questions.explanation: She proposes 'thirty-five percent on works priced under five thousand, forty on anything above'.
questions.hint: Listen for both percentages and the price threshold in her proposal.

questions.prompt: What flat rate does the Gallery Owner counter with?
questions.explanation: He offers 'thirty-eight flat' as a compromise.
questions.hint: Listen for the Gallery Owner's counter after the sliding scale proposal.

questions.prompt: What exhibition schedule does the Artist request in exchange for accepting 38%?
options: Two group shows and one solo in 18 months
One group show and two solos in 24 months
questions.explanation: She says 'a minimum of two group shows and one solo exhibition within the first eighteen months'.
questions.hint: Listen for the combined exhibition schedule the Artist puts as a condition.

questions.prompt: Why does the Gallery Owner find an 18-month solo show ambitious?
options: The gallery is fully booked for two years
They typically plan exhibitions 12–14 months in advance
The Artist's series is not ready
questions.explanation: He says 'we typically plan exhibitions twelve to fourteen months in advance'.
questions.hint: Listen for the lead time he mentions for exhibition planning.

questions.prompt: What revised timeline do they settle on for the solo show?
questions.explanation: The Artist revises to 'twenty-four months for the solo'.
questions.hint: Listen for the Artist's revised proposal after the Gallery Owner's pushback.

questions.prompt: What does the Gallery Owner want regarding the Artist's future work?
options: Exclusive rights to all future work forever
Right of first refusal on the next two bodies of work
The right to commission specific works
questions.explanation: He asks for 'a right of first refusal on your next two bodies of work'.
questions.hint: Listen for what the Gallery Owner asks about future work.

questions.prompt: Why does the Artist insist on retaining creative control?
options: She is legally required to do so
She has a bad experience of commercial pressure altering her practice
She does not trust curatorial experts
Her previous gallery forced her to change her style
questions.explanation: She says 'I've had experiences where commercial pressure shifted my practice in directions I regretted'.
questions.hint: Listen for the Artist's personal reason for wanting creative control.

questions.prompt: What does the Gallery Owner say he offers instead of dictating subject matter?
options: Marketing support and social media promotion
Curatorial guidance on scale, sequencing, and how work reads in the space
questions.explanation: He mentions 'curatorial guidance — suggestions about scale, sequencing, how a body of work reads in a white-cube context'.
questions.hint: Listen for the specific type of input the gallery says it provides.

questions.prompt: What three things does the Artist insist must remain solely her decision?
options: Subject matter, colour palette, and exhibition date
Pricing, collector choice, and opening events
questions.explanation: She says 'final decisions on titles, pricing, and display remain mine'.
questions.hint: Listen for the three specific rights the Artist wants written into the contract.

questions.prompt: Why does the Gallery Owner want pricing to be a joint decision?
options: Because he pays for the materials
Because pricing affects the gallery's market positioning
Because collectors demand to negotiate directly
Because the Artist consistently undervalues her work
questions.explanation: He says pricing 'affects the gallery's positioning in the market'.
questions.hint: Listen for the reason the Gallery Owner gives for wanting input on pricing.

questions.prompt: What condition does the Artist attach to agreeing on joint pricing?
options: A minimum guarantee of ten thousand dollars per work
A written price floor below which works won't be sold
An annual price review by an independent appraiser
The right to cancel any sale she disagrees with
questions.explanation: She asks for 'a written floor below which we won't sell regardless of market conditions'.
questions.hint: Listen for what protective mechanism the Artist requests around pricing.

questions.prompt: Why does the Gallery Owner agree a price floor is in his interest too?
options: It protects the resale value for existing collectors
It discourages rival galleries from competing
questions.explanation: He says 'it protects the resale value for existing collectors'.
questions.hint: Listen for the reason the Gallery Owner gives for welcoming the price floor.

questions.prompt: How many works per year does the Artist want the right to display outside the gallery without commission?
questions.explanation: She requests 'up to three works per year outside the gallery… without commission'.
questions.hint: Listen for the number the Artist mentions in her final request.

questions.prompt: What condition does the Gallery Owner attach to allowing non-commission external displays?
options: The Artist must give the gallery advance notice of 30 days
The works must carry gallery attribution
The works must be priced above the floor rate
The events must be in the same city as the gallery
questions.explanation: He says 'provided they carry gallery attribution'.
questions.hint: Listen for the single condition the Gallery Owner sets for external shows.

questions.prompt: Which phrase best describes the overall tone of this conversation?
options: Friendly but firm, with mutual respect
questions.explanation: Both parties make concessions, explain their reasoning, and reach agreement on each point without conflict.
questions.hint: Think about how each speaker responds to the other's proposals throughout.

cloze.template: The Artist began by acknowledging the gallery's {{b1}} for placing work with serious collectors. Her first concern was the {{b2}} rate of forty-five percent, which she described as {{b3}} given her material and studio costs. She proposed a {{b4}} scale: thirty-five percent under five thousand dollars, forty above. The Gallery Owner countered with a {{b5}} rate of thirty-eight percent. The Artist accepted this on condition that the gallery commit to a minimum of two {{b6}} shows and one {{b7}} exhibition. They agreed the solo show would happen within {{b8}} months. The Gallery Owner then asked for a right of first {{b9}} on the Artist's next two bodies of work. The Artist agreed but insisted on retaining {{b10}} control. She welcomed {{b11}} guidance but wanted titles, pricing, and {{b12}} to remain her decisions. They agreed on joint pricing but with a written {{b13}} below which works would not be sold. The Gallery Owner noted this also protects the {{b14}} value for collectors. Finally, the Artist requested the right to show up to three works outside the gallery annually without {{b15}}, provided the works carry gallery {{b16}}.

blanks.explanation: The quality that makes the gallery trusted.
The percentage the gallery takes from each sale.
A scale that changes based on price.
A show dedicated to one artist.
The agreed deadline for the solo exhibition.
The right to be offered something before others.
The way works are physically presented.
The price a work can achieve when sold on by a collector.
The percentage the gallery would otherwise take.

translationVi: Một nghệ sĩ người Mỹ và chủ phòng trưng bày người Anh đàm phán về tỷ lệ hoa hồng, lịch triển lãm, kiểm soát sáng tạo và quyền từ chối đầu tiên. Họ thỏa thuận mức hoa hồng cố định là 38%, một cuộc triển lãm solo trong vòng 24 tháng, một sàn giá bằng văn bản và quyền trưng bày ba tác phẩm bên ngoài phòng trưng bày hàng năm miễn phí hoa hồng với điều kiện ghi rõ tên phòng trưng bày.
criticalThinkingQuestion: The Artist and Gallery Owner reach compromises on every point. Do you think the Artist gave up too much to secure the deal, or did she negotiate effectively? Which concession was most significant, and was there anything she should have refused to compromise on?
